frameworks.name: Wiener-Granger causality
frameworks.one_line: Directional information flow between regions, extended to be feature-specific.

frameworks.name: Information theory
frameworks.one_line: Quantifies feature-specific content of inter-region communication.

core_question: How can one quantify the amount, content, and direction of information about a specific feature transmitted between brain regions?
key_assumptions: Information flow can be decomposed into feature-specific components
Simultaneously recorded signals from two regions support directed inference
Wiener-Granger causality principle applies to neural activity
manipulations: Simulated neural activity with controlled feature transmission
Applied measure across MEG, EEG, and spiking datasets
Compared FIT against traditional Granger-style measures
key_findings: FIT isolates feature-specific information within total inter-region flow in simulations
FIT recovers content and direction of communication in MEG, EEG, and spiking data
Analytical proofs establish key properties of the measure
interpretations: Merging Wiener-Granger causality with information-content specificity reveals what feature is communicated, not just whether
FIT exposes flows invisible to traditional analytical methods
